title: Português de Portugal rumo ao DIPLE

days.title: Os sons do português
days.objective: You will recognise and produce the signature sounds of European Portuguese: nasal ão/õe, lh/nh, the sh-like final -s and vowel reduction.
days.paretoFocus: European Portuguese sounds nothing like it looks on paper: master the nasal vowels, lh/nh and the 'sh' final -s once, and both listening and reading unlock at the same time.
drills: Say não, mãe, pão aloud five times, letting the air go through your nose.
Whisper 'dois, boas, olhos' and listen for the 'sh' at the end of each word.
Read 'senhora' and 'filho' aloud, exaggerating nh and lh.

items: Say the nasal diphthong ão in três words: não, pão, mão.
Recall: how does final -s sound in European Portuguese?
Read 'Olá, bom dia!' aloud with relaxed unstressed vowels.

activities.title: Os sons-chave
activities.intro: European Portuguese has a small set of sounds that carry most of the accent. Say each example aloud — through the nose for the nasals, and let unstressed vowels almost vanish.

items.en: nasal diphthong 'ão' — no, bread, hand
items.note: Air through the nose; the most Portuguese sound of all.

items.en: nasal 'õe' — (he/she) puts, lemons
items.note: Plural of many -ão words: limão → limões.

items.en: nasal 'ã' — mother, tomorrow, sister
'lh' — son, woman, work
items.note: Like the 'lli' in English 'million'.

items.en: 'nh' — madam, tomorrow, wine
items.note: Like the 'ny' in 'canyon'.

items.en: final -s — two, good (fem. pl.), eyes
items.note: At the end of a word or before a consonant, -s sounds like English 'sh': doish, boash.

items.en: reduced vowels — telephone, night, seven
items.note: Unstressed 'e' and 'o' shrink or vanish: t'lefone, noit', set'.

activities.title: Ouvir com os olhos
activities.intro: Choose or complete. Say each word aloud before answering.

questions.prompt: Which word contains the nasal diphthong 'ão'?
questions.explanation: não has the nasal diphthong ão; the others are oral vowels.

questions.prompt: In European Portuguese, the final -s of 'dois' sounds like…
questions.explanation: Word-final -s (and -s before a consonant) is pronounced like 'sh': doish.

questions.prompt: The 'lh' in 'filho' sounds closest to…
questions.explanation: lh is a palatal 'l', like the middle of 'million'.

questions.prompt: Which word has the same 'nh' sound as 'senhora'?
questions.explanation: vinho (wine) also has the palatal 'ny' sound written nh.

questions.prompt: In European Portuguese, the unstressed 'e' sounds in 'telefone' are…
questions.explanation: Vowel reduction: unstressed e/o shrink — t'lefone.

questions.prompt: Complete with the nasal ending: p___ (bread). Type only the missing letters.
questions.explanation: pão (bread) ends in the nasal diphthong ão.

questions.prompt: Complete with the two letters that make the 'canyon' sound: mi___a (my, feminine). Type only the two letters.
questions.explanation: minha (my, feminine) uses nh, the same palatal sound as senhora.

activities.title: Primeiro contacto
activities.intro: A minimal first exchange. Read both parts aloud — nasals through the nose, final -s as 'sh' — then read only the 'Me' lines.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Olá! Bom dia!
lines.en: Hello! Good morning!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Olá, bom dia!
lines.en: Hello, good morning!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Como está?
lines.en: How are you? (formal)

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bem, obrigado. E a senhora?
lines.en: Well, thank you. And you, madam?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Muito bem, obrigada.
lines.en: Very well, thank you. (said by a woman)

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Até logo!
lines.en: See you later!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Adeus!
lines.en: Goodbye!

days.title: Cumprimentos
days.objective: You will greet, thank and apologise correctly at any time of day, including the obrigado/obrigada rule.
days.paretoFocus: Greetings and courtesy words open every single conversation in Portugal; getting bom dia/boa tarde/boa noite and obrigado/obrigada right instantly marks you as someone who cares.
drills: Say the right greeting for right now (morning, afternoon or night).
Say 'obrigado' or 'obrigada' according to YOUR gender, five times.
Role-play entering a café: greeting + se faz favor + thanks + goodbye.

items: Which greeting do you use at 15:00? And at 22:00?
Who says 'obrigada' — men or women?
Recall two ways to say 'please' in Portugal.

activities.title: Cumprimentos e cortesia
activities.intro: The courtesy toolkit you will use every day. Note the time boundaries of the greetings and the gender rule for thanking.

items.en: Hello! (any time, informal or neutral)
Good morning! (until about lunchtime)
Good afternoon! (after lunch until dark)
Good evening! / Good night! (after dark)
thank you
items.note: Agrees with the SPEAKER: a man says obrigado, a woman says obrigada — always.

items.en: please
items.note: 'se faz favor' (often written s.f.f.) is very common in Portugal.

items.en: sorry / excuse me (formal)
items.note: Use it to apologise or to get someone's attention.

items.en: goodbye / see you later / see you tomorrow

activities.title: Cortesia em ação
activities.intro: Pick the right courtesy word for each situation.

questions.prompt: It is 16:00. Which greeting do you use?
questions.explanation: From after lunch until nightfall, the greeting is boa tarde.

questions.prompt: A woman receives a coffee. She says…
questions.explanation: obrigado/obrigada agrees with the speaker: women say obrigada.

questions.prompt: A very Portuguese way to say 'please' is…
questions.explanation: se faz favor (s.f.f.) is heard constantly in Portugal, alongside por favor.

questions.prompt: 'Até logo' means…
questions.explanation: até logo = see you later (same day); até amanhã = see you tomorrow.

questions.prompt: You bump into someone on the autocarro. You say: ______ (sorry, formal).
questions.explanation: Desculpe is the formal apology and attention-getter.

questions.prompt: It is 22:00 and you enter a restaurant. You greet: Boa ______!
questions.explanation: After dark the greeting is boa noite.

questions.prompt: Build the Portuguese for: 'Good morning, how are you?'
questions.explanation: Greeting first, then the polite question como está?

activities.title: No café
activities.intro: Ordering with full courtesy. Read aloud, then cover the English and read only your lines.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Boa tarde! Faz favor?
lines.en: Good afternoon! What can I get you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Boa tarde! Um café, se faz favor.
lines.en: Good afternoon! An espresso, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Com certeza. Mais alguma coisa?
lines.en: Certainly. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Não, obrigado.
lines.en: No, thank you. (said by a man)

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Aqui está. Bom dia para si!
lines.en: Here you are. Have a good day!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Obrigado, igualmente. Até logo!
lines.en: Thanks, likewise. See you later!

days.title: Apresentações
days.objective: You will introduce yourself — name, origin, where you live — using natural European word order with ênclise (Chamo-me…).
days.paretoFocus: Self-introduction is the first real conversation you will ever have; learning 'Chamo-me' with the pronoun AFTER the verb builds the European Portuguese reflex from day one.
drills: Say your own full introduction aloud: Chamo-me… Sou de… Moro em…
Ask the mirror: Como se chama? and answer yourself.
Swap in three different cities after 'Moro em…'.

items: Where does the pronoun go in 'Chamo-me'?
Recall the difference between 'Sou de…' and 'Moro em…'.
What do you reply to 'Muito prazer'?

activities.title: Apresentar-se
activities.intro: In European Portuguese the pronoun normally comes AFTER the verb, joined by a hyphen (ênclise): Chamo-me, not the other way round. Learn these as fixed chunks.

items.en: My name is Ana.
items.note: Ênclise: verb + hyphen + pronoun. This order is the European default.

items.en: What is your name? (formal)
What is your name? (informal, tu)
I am from Lisbon.
items.note: Origin: ser + de.

items.en: I am Portuguese. (man / woman)
I live in Coimbra.
items.note: morar em = to live in. Some cities take an article: Moro no Porto.

items.en: Nice to meet you. — Likewise.

items.hr: Este é o meu amigo João.
items.en: This is my friend João.

activities.title: Quem és?
activities.intro: Build correct European introductions.

questions.prompt: The natural European Portuguese order is…
questions.explanation: In Portugal the pronoun attaches after the verb with a hyphen: Chamo-me.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form: ______-me Sofia. (chamar)
questions.explanation: chamar → (eu) chamo; with the pronoun: Chamo-me Sofia.

questions.prompt: 'Sou de Braga.' tells people…
questions.explanation: ser + de = origin; morar em = current residence.

questions.prompt: Someone says 'Muito prazer.' You reply…
questions.explanation: Muito prazer → Igualmente (likewise).

questions.prompt: Complete: ______ no Porto. (morar)
questions.explanation: morar → (eu) moro; Porto takes the article: no Porto.

questions.prompt: Build the Portuguese for: 'My name is Ana and I'm from Lisbon.'
questions.explanation: Name first (Chamo-me Ana), then origin (sou de Lisboa).

questions.prompt: To ask a stranger's name formally, you say…
questions.explanation: Formal: Como se chama? Informal (tu): Como te chamas?

activities.title: Muito prazer
activities.intro: Two people meet at a course in Lisbon. Read both roles aloud.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Olá! Como te chamas?
lines.en: Hi! What's your name?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Chamo-me Ricardo. E tu?
lines.en: My name is Ricardo. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Chamo-me Inês. Muito prazer!
lines.en: My name is Inês. Nice to meet you!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Igualmente! És de Lisboa?
lines.en: Likewise! Are you from Lisbon?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Não, sou de Coimbra, mas moro em Lisboa. E tu, de onde és?
lines.en: No, I'm from Coimbra, but I live in Lisbon. And you, where are you from?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sou do México e agora moro no Porto.
lines.en: I'm from Mexico and now I live in Porto.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Que bom! Bem-vindo a Portugal!
lines.en: How nice! Welcome to Portugal!

days.title: Ser e estar
days.objective: You will use ser for identity and origin and estar for states and location, including the European 'estar a + infinitivo' for actions in progress.
days.paretoFocus: Portuguese splits English 'to be' in two; choosing ser vs estar correctly is the single highest-value grammar habit at A1, and 'estar a fazer' is how Portugal says what is happening right now.
drills: Say three permanent facts about yourself with ser (Sou…).
Say three temporary states with estar (Estou…).
Describe what you are doing right now with 'Estou a + infinitivo'.

items: Which verb for identity and origin — ser or estar?
Which verb for feelings and location right now?
Say 'I am working (right now)' the European way.

activities.title: Dois verbos 'to be'
activities.intro: ser = what something IS (identity, origin, profession). estar = how/where something is NOW (state, location, action in progress). In Portugal, ongoing actions use estar a + infinitivo.

items.hr: ser: sou, és, é, somos, são
items.en: to be (permanent): I am, you are, he/she is, we are, they are

items.hr: estar: estou, estás, está, estamos, estão
items.en: to be (temporary): I am, you are, he/she is, we are, they are
I am a teacher. I am from Faro.
items.note: Identity, profession, origin → ser.

items.en: I am tired. (man / woman)
items.note: Temporary state → estar.

items.en: The coffee is hot.
items.note: Condition right now → estar.

items.en: I am at home.
items.note: Location of people/things → estar.

items.en: I am working (right now).
items.note: European Portuguese: estar a + infinitivo for actions in progress.

items.en: Ana is nice (as a person).
items.note: Personality trait → ser. Note the article before names: a Ana.

activities.title: Ser ou estar?
activities.intro: Choose the right 'to be' and the right form.

questions.prompt: Eu ______ português.
questions.explanation: Nationality is identity → ser: sou português.

questions.prompt: O chá ______ quente.
questions.explanation: The tea's temperature right now → estar: está quente.

questions.prompt: Which sentence says 'I am studying (right now)' the European way?
questions.explanation: estar a + infinitivo: Estou a estudar.

questions.prompt: Nós ______ cansados hoje. (estar)
questions.explanation: Temporary state, nós → estamos.

questions.prompt: Tu ______ de Braga? (ser)
questions.explanation: Origin → ser; tu → és.

questions.prompt: For identity, profession and origin, Portuguese uses…
questions.explanation: ser = permanent identity; estar = temporary state or location.

questions.prompt: Build the Portuguese for: 'She is working right now.'
questions.explanation: estar a + infinitivo: está a trabalhar = is working.

activities.title: Como estás?
activities.intro: A phone call between friends. Notice ser for facts and estar for states and actions.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Olá! Como estás?
lines.en: Hi! How are you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Estou bem, mas estou um pouco cansado. E tu?
lines.en: I'm well, but I'm a bit tired. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Estou ótima! Estás em casa?
lines.en: I'm great! Are you at home?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Não, estou no escritório. Estou a trabalhar.
lines.en: No, I'm at the office. I'm working.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ainda? Já é tarde!
lines.en: Still? It's late already!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Eu sei… O trabalho é assim. Falamos logo!
lines.en: I know… Work is like that. Let's talk later!

days.title: Números e horas
days.objective: You will count from 0 to 100 and ask and tell the time with 'Que horas são?'.
days.paretoFocus: Numbers and clock time run prices, schedules, comboio departures and appointments — a small set you will use dozens of times a day in Portugal.
drills: Count aloud 0–20 without looking.
Say the tens: vinte, trinta, quarenta… cem.
Look at a clock three times today and say the time in Portuguese.

items: Count 11–19 aloud.
How do you ask the time?
Say 2:30 and 1:00 in Portuguese — one uses São, the other É.

activities.title: Números e o relógio
activities.intro: Learn the building blocks: 0–15 by heart, then the tens. For time: São + hours (plural), but É uma hora / É meio-dia (singular).

items.hr: zero, um, dois, três, quatro, cinco

items.note: European spelling: dezasseis, dezassete, dezanove.

items.hr: trinta, quarenta, cinquenta, sessenta, setenta, oitenta, noventa, cem
items.note: Combine with e: vinte e um (21), trinta e cinco (35).

items.en: What time is it?

items.hr: São três horas. / São três e meia.
items.en: It's three o'clock. / It's half past three.
items.note: e meia = half past; e um quarto = quarter past.

items.hr: É uma hora. / É meio-dia. / É meia-noite.
items.en: It's one o'clock. / It's midday. / It's midnight.
items.note: Singular É with uma hora, meio-dia, meia-noite.

activities.title: Contar e dizer as horas
activities.intro: Numbers first, then the clock.

questions.prompt: Which is the number 15?
questions.explanation: 15 = quinze; 5 = cinco; 50 = cinquenta; 14 = catorze.

questions.prompt: The European spelling of 17 is…
questions.explanation: In Portugal: dezasseis, dezassete, dezanove (with a).

questions.prompt: It is 3:30. You say…
questions.explanation: Plural São + hour + e meia for half past.

questions.prompt: It is 1:00. You say…
questions.explanation: One o'clock is singular: É uma hora (hora is feminine → uma).

questions.prompt: Write the number 7 in words.
questions.explanation: 7 = sete.

questions.prompt: Write the number 40 in words.
questions.explanation: 40 = quarenta; 4 = quatro; 14 = catorze.

questions.prompt: Build the Portuguese for: 'What time is it, please?'
questions.explanation: The polite way to ask the time on the street.

activities.title: A que horas parte o comboio?
activities.intro: At the train station. Numbers and times everywhere — read the times aloud.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Desculpe, que horas são?
lines.en: Excuse me, what time is it?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: São duas e um quarto.
lines.en: It's a quarter past two.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Obrigado. A que horas parte o comboio para o Porto?
lines.en: Thank you. What time does the train to Porto leave?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Parte às três e meia, da linha cinco.
lines.en: It leaves at half past three, from platform five.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: E quanto custa o bilhete?
lines.en: And how much does the ticket cost?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Vinte e cinco euros.
lines.en: Twenty-five euros.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perfeito, obrigado!
lines.en: Perfect, thank you!

days.title: Género e artigos
days.objective: You will match o/a/um/uma to nouns and use the everyday contractions do, da, no, na, ao, à inside real phrases.
days.paretoFocus: Almost every Portuguese sentence contains an article, and prepositions fuse with them (de+o=do, em+a=na, a+a=à); automating these contractions makes your Portuguese flow instead of stutter.
drills: Say five nouns with the right article: o/a + noun.
Chant the contractions: do-da, no-na, ao-à.
Make three real sentences: Vou ao…, Moro na…, Venho do…

items: de + o = ? and em + a = ?
Say 'I'm going to the beach' with the contraction à.
Which article goes with 'casa' and which with 'livro'?

activities.title: Artigos e contrações
activities.intro: Every noun is masculine or feminine: o livro, a casa. Prepositions de/em/a FUSE with the article — never say 'de o'; always do. Learn the fused forms inside real phrases.

items.en: the book / the house
items.note: Most -o nouns are masculine, most -a nouns feminine.

items.en: a coffee / a table

items.hr: do = de + o — Venho do Porto.
items.en: of/from the — I come from Porto.
items.note: Porto takes the article: o Porto → do Porto.

items.hr: da = de + a — o centro da cidade
items.en: of the — the centre of the city

items.hr: no = em + o — Trabalho no banco.
items.en: in/at the — I work at the bank.

items.hr: na = em + a — Moro na Baixa.
items.en: in the — I live in the Baixa (downtown).

items.hr: ao = a + o — Vou ao cinema.
items.en: to the — I'm going to the cinema.

items.hr: à = a + a — Vou à praia.
items.en: to the — I'm going to the beach.
items.note: The grave accent on à marks the fusion a + a.

activities.title: o, a, do, na, à…
activities.intro: Pick or build the right article or contraction.

questions.prompt: ______ casa é grande.
questions.explanation: casa is feminine → a casa.

questions.prompt: de + o = …
questions.explanation: de fuses with o to give do: Venho do Porto.

questions.prompt: Moro ______ Porto.
questions.explanation: Porto takes the masculine article: em + o Porto = no Porto.

questions.prompt: Which noun takes 'uma'?
questions.explanation: mesa is feminine → uma mesa; livro, café and dia are masculine.

questions.prompt: Vou ______ praia. (a + a, one accented letter)
questions.explanation: a (to) + a (the, fem.) = à: Vou à praia.

questions.prompt: Venho ______ escritório. (de + o)
questions.explanation: de + o = do: Venho do escritório.

questions.prompt: Build the Portuguese for: 'I'm going to the cinema with a friend.'
questions.explanation: a + o cinema = ao cinema; com um amigo = with a friend.

activities.title: Onde fica?
activities.intro: Asking about places in town. Every line hides a contraction — spot them all.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Desculpe, onde fica a estação do metro?
lines.en: Excuse me, where is the metro station?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Fica no centro, ao lado do banco.
lines.en: It's in the centre, next to the bank.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: É longe da praça?
lines.en: Is it far from the square?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Não, é perto. Vai à praça e vira na primeira rua.
lines.en: No, it's close. Go to the square and turn at the first street.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: E há um café na estação?
lines.en: And is there a café at the station?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Há, sim. O café da estação é muito bom.
lines.en: There is, yes. The station café is very good.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ótimo, obrigado!
lines.en: Great, thank you!

days.title: A família
days.objective: You will name family members and say whose they are with o meu/a minha and o teu/a tua.
days.paretoFocus: Family is the first personal topic in any Portuguese conversation, and it forces the key European habit: article + possessive (o meu irmão, a minha mãe).
drills: Name five members of your real family in Portuguese.
Say each one with a possessive: o meu…, a minha…
Ask aloud: E tu, tens irmãos?

items: What does 'os pais' mean — careful, it's not 'the fathers'?
Say 'my mother' and 'my brother' with the article.
avó or avô — which one is grandmother?

activities.title: A minha família
activities.intro: Family words come in masculine/feminine pairs. In European Portuguese, possessives normally carry an article: o meu pai, a minha mãe.

items.en: father / mother
items.note: os pais = parents (not 'fathers').

items.en: brother / sister
items.note: Plural: os irmãos can mean brothers OR siblings.

items.en: son / daughter
grandfather / grandmother
items.note: Only the accent differs: avô (ô, grandpa) vs avó (ó, grandma).

items.en: husband / wife

items.hr: o meu irmão / a minha irmã
items.en: my brother / my sister
items.note: Article + possessive is the European norm: o meu, a minha.

items.hr: o teu pai / a tua mãe
items.en: your father / your mother (informal, tu)
Do you have siblings?

activities.title: Quem é quem?
activities.intro: Family words and possessives.

questions.prompt: 'os pais' means…
questions.explanation: os pais = parents (pai + mãe together).

questions.prompt: ______ minha mãe chama-se Teresa.
questions.explanation: European Portuguese uses article + possessive: A minha mãe.

questions.prompt: Grandmother is…
questions.explanation: avó (ó) = grandmother; avô (ô) = grandfather.

questions.prompt: The feminine of 'o filho' is…
questions.explanation: filho → filha: son → daughter.

questions.prompt: O meu ______ chama-se Pedro. (brother)
questions.explanation: brother = irmão (with the nasal ão).

questions.prompt: A ______ filha é simpática. (your, informal, feminine)
questions.explanation: tu → teu/tua; filha is feminine → a tua filha.

questions.prompt: Build the Portuguese for: 'My sister lives in Faro.'
questions.explanation: Article + possessive + noun: A minha irmã.

activities.title: Tens irmãos?
activities.intro: Two colleagues talk about family over coffee.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tens irmãos?
lines.en: Do you have siblings?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tenho, sim. Tenho um irmão e uma irmã.
lines.en: I do, yes. I have a brother and a sister.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E onde moram?
lines.en: And where do they live?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: O meu irmão mora em Lisboa e a minha irmã mora no estrangeiro.
lines.en: My brother lives in Lisbon and my sister lives abroad.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E os teus pais?
lines.en: And your parents?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Os meus pais moram no Algarve, perto da minha avó.
lines.en: My parents live in the Algarve, near my grandmother.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Que família grande e bonita!
lines.en: What a big, lovely family!

days.title: Verbos em -AR
days.objective: You will conjugate regular -AR verbs (falar, morar, trabalhar, estudar, gostar) in the present tense and use gostar DE correctly.
days.paretoFocus: -AR is the biggest verb family in Portuguese: one ending pattern (o, as, a, amos, am) instantly unlocks hundreds of verbs, and 'gostar de' lets you talk about everything you like.
drills: Chant the endings: -o, -as, -a, -amos, -am.
Conjugate morar and trabalhar aloud, all persons.
Say three true sentences with 'Gosto de…'.

items: Recite falar in the present: falo, falas…
Which little word must always follow gostar?
Say 'we work' and 'they study' in Portuguese.

activities.title: O padrão -AR
activities.intro: Remove -ar, add the endings: -o, -as, -a, -amos, -am. The same pattern works for hundreds of verbs. gostar always takes de before its object.

items.hr: falar: falo, falas, fala, falamos, falam
items.en: to speak: I speak, you speak, he/she speaks, we speak, they speak
I speak Portuguese and English.
to live — Do you live in Lisbon?
to work — We work at a school.
items.note: numa = em + uma.

items.en: to study — They study in the evening.
to like — I like coffee.
items.note: gostar ALWAYS takes de: gosto de música, gostas de ler.

items.en: Do you like travelling? (informal)

items.hr: A Rita fala com a mãe ao telemóvel.
items.en: Rita talks with her mother on the mobile phone.

activities.title: Conjugar -AR
activities.intro: Apply the -AR pattern. The infinitive in brackets tells you which verb to conjugate.

questions.prompt: Eu ______ português todos os dias. (falar)
questions.explanation: eu → -o: falo.

questions.prompt: Nós ______ numa escola. (trabalhar)
questions.explanation: nós → -amos: trabalhamos.

questions.prompt: gostar is always followed by…
questions.explanation: gostar de: Gosto de café, gostas de música.

questions.prompt: Tu ______ em Lisboa? (morar)
questions.explanation: tu → -as: moras.

questions.prompt: Ela ______ de música portuguesa. (gostar)
questions.explanation: ela → -a: gosta de música.

questions.prompt: Eles ______ português à noite. (estudar)
questions.explanation: eles → -am: estudam.

questions.prompt: Build the Portuguese for: 'I like coffee with milk.'
questions.explanation: gostar de + noun: gosto de café com leite.

activities.title: O que gostas de fazer?
activities.intro: Two new friends compare daily lives. Every verb is from the -AR family.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Onde trabalhas?
lines.en: Where do you work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Trabalho num escritório no centro. E tu?
lines.en: I work in an office in the centre. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Eu estudo na universidade e trabalho à noite.
lines.en: I study at university and work in the evening.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: E o que gostas de fazer ao fim de semana?
lines.en: And what do you like doing at the weekend?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gosto de caminhar na praia e de falar com amigos.
lines.en: I like walking on the beach and talking with friends.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Eu também gosto muito da praia!
lines.en: I also really like the beach!

days.title: Verbos em -ER e -IR
days.objective: You will conjugate regular -ER and -IR verbs (comer, beber, viver, abrir, partir) and meet the essential irregulars saber (sei) and poder (posso).
days.paretoFocus: With -ER and -IR you complete all three regular verb families — and saber/poder ('I know / I can') are among the most spoken verbs in the entire language.
drills: Chant comer: como, comes, come, comemos, comem.
Chant abrir: abro, abres, abre, abrimos, abrem.
Say one thing you know (Sei…) and one thing you can do (Posso…).

items: How do -ER and -IR endings differ in the nós form?
Irregular eu-forms: saber → ? and poder → ?
Say 'the train leaves at eight' in Portuguese.

activities.title: -ER, -IR e dois irregulares
activities.intro: -ER: -o, -es, -e, -emos, -em. -IR: same except nós → -imos. Then two heavyweights with irregular eu-forms: saber → sei, poder → posso.

items.hr: comer: como, comes, come, comemos, comem
items.en: to eat: I eat, you eat, he/she eats, we eat, they eat
to drink — Do you drink water or wine?
to live — We live in Portugal.

items.hr: abrir: abro, abres, abre, abrimos, abrem
items.en: to open: I open, you open, he/she opens, we open, they open
items.note: -IR pattern: only the nós form (-imos) differs from -ER.

items.hr: partir — O comboio parte às oito.
items.en: to leave/depart — The train leaves at eight.
to know — I know how to speak English.
items.note: Irregular eu-form: sei (not 'sabo').

items.en: can / to be able — May I come in?
items.note: Irregular eu-form: posso; tu podes, ele pode.

items.en: I eat bread at breakfast.
items.note: pequeno-almoço = breakfast in Portugal.

activities.title: Conjugar -ER e -IR
activities.intro: Regular patterns plus the irregular eu-forms sei and posso.

questions.prompt: Eu ______ pão ao pequeno-almoço. (comer)
questions.explanation: eu → -o: como.

questions.prompt: The irregular eu-form of saber is…
questions.explanation: saber is irregular in the first person: eu sei.

questions.prompt: Nós ______ em Portugal. (viver)
questions.explanation: -ER verbs take -emos in the nós form: vivemos.

questions.prompt: Tu ______ água? (beber)
questions.explanation: tu → -es: bebes.

questions.prompt: Eu ______ falar três línguas. (poder... careful, irregular!)
questions.explanation: poder → eu posso (irregular).

questions.prompt: Ela ______ a janela. (abrir)
questions.explanation: ela → -e: abre.

questions.prompt: Build the Portuguese for: 'The train leaves at eight o'clock.'
questions.explanation: partir → parte; às oito horas = at eight o'clock.

activities.title: Ao pequeno-almoço
activities.intro: Morning at a guesthouse. -ER/-IR verbs plus saber and poder in action.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bom dia! O que comes ao pequeno-almoço?
lines.en: Good morning! What do you eat for breakfast?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Como pão com queijo e bebo um galão.
lines.en: I eat bread with cheese and drink a galão (milky coffee).

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sabes a que horas abre a padaria?
lines.en: Do you know what time the bakery opens?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sei, sim. Abre às sete.
lines.en: I do, yes. It opens at seven.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Podes comprar pão para nós?
lines.en: Can you buy bread for us?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Posso, claro. O autocarro parte às nove, temos tempo.
lines.en: I can, of course. The bus leaves at nine, we have time.

days.title: Perguntas
days.objective: You will ask questions with o que, quem, onde, quando, como, quanto and porquê, including the very Portuguese 'é que' pattern.
days.paretoFocus: Questions drive every conversation; seven question words plus rising intonation turn everything you learned this week into real two-way dialogue.
drills: Read the seven question words aloud twice.
Turn 'Moras em Lisboa.' into a question using only intonation.
Ask three real questions with 'é que': Onde é que…? Quando é que…?

items: Which word asks about a person? And about a place?
porquê or porque — which one stands alone at the end?
Ask 'How much does it cost?' in Portuguese.

activities.title: As palavras interrogativas
activities.intro: Seven words open almost every question. Portuguese also loves the filler 'é que' after the question word (Onde é que moras?) — and a simple rising intonation turns any statement into a question.

items.hr: O que…? — O que é isto?
items.en: What…? — What is this?
Who…? — Who is she?
Where…? — Where do you live?
When…? — When do you arrive?
How…? — What (how) are you called?
How much…? — How much does it cost?
items.note: Agrees with the noun: quanto tempo, quanta água, quantos anos.

items.en: Why? / because — Why? Because I like it!
items.note: Alone or at the end: porquê (with accent). Introducing a clause: porque.

items.en: Where (is it that) you live?
items.note: 'é que' after the question word is extremely common in Portugal.

activities.title: Fazer perguntas
activities.intro: Choose or build the right question word.

questions.prompt: ______ custa o bilhete?
questions.explanation: Price → quanto: Quanto custa?

questions.prompt: To ask about a PLACE, you use…
questions.explanation: onde = where; quem = who; quando = when.

questions.prompt: Standing alone at the end of a sentence, 'why' is written…
questions.explanation: Alone or final → porquê (accented); porque introduces the reason.

questions.prompt: 'Quem' asks about…
questions.explanation: quem = who: Quem é ela?

questions.prompt: ______ é que te chamas? (how)
questions.explanation: Como é que te chamas? — the é que pattern is very Portuguese.

questions.prompt: ______ horas são? (what, before a noun)
questions.explanation: Before a noun use que: Que horas são?

questions.prompt: Build the Portuguese for: 'Where do you live?'
questions.explanation: Question word + é que + verb: the everyday European pattern.

activities.title: Tantas perguntas!
activities.intro: A curious new friend fires off questions — answer them all, then swap roles aloud.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Onde é que moras?
lines.en: Where do you live?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Moro no Porto, perto do rio.
lines.en: I live in Porto, near the river.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E o que fazes?
lines.en: And what do you do?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Trabalho num hospital. E tu, quando é que começas o novo trabalho?
lines.en: I work at a hospital. And you, when do you start the new job?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Na segunda-feira. Porquê?
lines.en: On Monday. Why?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Porque quero celebrar contigo! Quanto custa um jantar bom aqui?
lines.en: Because I want to celebrate with you! How much does a good dinner cost here?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Não sei, mas o restaurante da praça é ótimo!
lines.en: I don't know, but the restaurant on the square is great!

days.title: No café
days.objective: Order drinks and snacks in a Portuguese café and ask for the bill politely.
days.paretoFocus: One frame — 'Queria X, se faz favor' — plus a handful of café words (bica, galão, tosta, pastel de nata) covers almost every café interaction in Portugal.
drills: Order three different things aloud with 'Queria..., se faz favor'.
Say the Lisbon café words: bica, galão, tosta mista, sandes.
Review today's words in the Words tab.

items: Order a coffee and a pastel de nata aloud.
Ask for the bill.
Recall: what is 'uma bica' in Lisbon?

activities.title: No café
activities.intro: Café culture is central in Portugal. The magic ordering frame is 'Queria... , se faz favor' (I'd like..., please). Note the Lisbon word 'bica' for espresso.

items.hr: Queria um galão, se faz favor.
items.en: I'd like a galão (tall milky coffee), please.
an espresso — 'bica' is the Lisbon word; elsewhere just 'um café'
a custard tart — Portugal's famous pastry
a toasted ham-and-cheese sandwich
a cheese sandwich — 'sandes' is the everyday Portuguese word
an orange juice
The bill, please.

activities.title: No café
activities.intro: Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: In a Lisbon café, 'uma bica' is…
questions.explanation: 'Bica' is the Lisbon word for a short black espresso.

questions.prompt: The most natural polite way to order:
options: Queria um galão, se faz favor.
questions.answer: Queria um galão, se faz favor.
questions.explanation: 'Queria... , se faz favor' is the standard polite ordering frame.

questions.prompt: 'Se faz favor' means…
questions.explanation: 'Se faz favor' (or 'por favor') = please; very common in Portugal.

questions.prompt: Ask for the bill: 'A ____, se faz favor.' (the word for the bill)
questions.explanation: A conta, se faz favor. = The bill, please.

questions.prompt: Order the toasted ham-and-cheese: 'Queria uma ____ mista, se faz favor.'
questions.explanation: Uma tosta mista = a toasted ham-and-cheese sandwich.

questions.prompt: 'Uma sandes' is…
questions.explanation: 'Sandes' is the everyday European Portuguese word for sandwich.

questions.prompt: Put in order: 'I'd like a custard tart, please.'
questions.explanation: Queria um pastel de nata, se faz favor.

activities.title: No café
activities.intro: You order breakfast at a café in Lisbon. Read both parts aloud, then just the 'Me' lines.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bom dia! O que deseja?
lines.en: Good morning! What would you like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bom dia. Queria um galão e um pastel de nata, se faz favor.
lines.en: Good morning. I'd like a galão and a custard tart, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Com certeza. Mais alguma coisa?
lines.en: Of course. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sim, também queria uma tosta mista.
lines.en: Yes, I'd also like a toasted ham-and-cheese sandwich.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Muito bem. É para comer aqui?
lines.en: Very well. Is it to eat here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sim, é para aqui. Obrigado.
lines.en: Yes, it's for here. Thank you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Aqui está. Bom apetite!
lines.en: Here you are. Enjoy!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Obrigado. A conta, se faz favor.
lines.en: Thank you. The bill, please.

days.title: Gostos
days.objective: Say what you like, love, prefer and hate, using 'gostar de' correctly with foods and activities.
days.paretoFocus: Four verbs — gostar de, adorar, preferir, detestar — express almost every opinion. The trap: 'gostar' always needs 'de'.
drills: Say three things you like with 'Gosto de...'.
Say one thing you love and one you hate.
Review today's words in the Words tab.

items: Recall: which preposition follows 'gostar'?
Say 'I prefer tea to coffee' aloud.
Order politely at a café (yesterday's frame).

activities.title: Gostar, adorar, preferir, detestar
activities.intro: 'Gostar' always takes 'de' before what you like — a noun or a verb in the infinitive. 'Adorar', 'preferir' and 'detestar' take the thing directly.

items.en: I like coffee. (gostar DE + noun)
I don't like fish.
I really like cooking. (gostar de + infinitive)
I love custard tarts. (adorar — no 'de')
I prefer tea to coffee. (preferir X a Y)
I hate onion.
Do you like cod? (informal 'tu' form)

activities.title: Gostos
activities.intro: Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: Complete with the preposition that always follows 'gostar': 'Gosto ____ chocolate.'
questions.explanation: Gostar always takes 'de': gosto de chocolate.

questions.prompt: 'Detesto' means…
questions.explanation: Detestar = to hate; the opposite of adorar.

questions.prompt: Ask a friend: '____ de fruta?' (gostar, 'tu' form)
questions.explanation: Tu form: gostas. 'Gostas de fruta?' = Do you like fruit?

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: Before an infinitive, gostar still needs plain 'de': gosto de nadar.

questions.prompt: Put in order: 'I prefer tea to coffee.'
questions.explanation: Preferir X a Y: Prefiro chá a café.

questions.prompt: 'Ele ____ de queijo.' (gostar, 'ele' form)
questions.explanation: Ele/ela form: gosta. Ele gosta de queijo = He likes cheese.

questions.prompt: 'Adoro bacalhau' expresses…
options: a stronger feeling than 'gosto de bacalhau'
a weaker feeling than 'gosto de bacalhau'
questions.answer: a stronger feeling than 'gosto de bacalhau'
questions.explanation: Adorar = to love; stronger than gostar de.

activities.title: O que gostas de comer?
activities.intro: Two friends talk about food. Read both parts aloud, then just the 'Me' lines.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gostas de peixe?
lines.en: Do you like fish?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gosto muito. Adoro bacalhau.
lines.en: I like it a lot. I love cod.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Eu prefiro carne. E detesto cebola!
lines.en: I prefer meat. And I hate onion!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A sério? Eu gosto de cebola.
lines.en: Really? I like onion.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E de sobremesa, o que preferes?
lines.en: And for dessert, what do you prefer?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Prefiro fruta, mas adoro pastéis de nata.
lines.en: I prefer fruit, but I love custard tarts.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Toda a gente adora pastéis de nata!
lines.en: Everyone loves custard tarts!

days.title: Na cidade
days.objective: Name key places in town, ask where something is with 'Onde fica...?', and understand basic directions.
days.paretoFocus: One question — 'Onde fica...?' — plus perto/longe, à direita/à esquerda and 'sempre em frente' lets you navigate any Portuguese town.
drills: Ask 'Onde fica...?' about three places aloud.
Point and say: à direita, à esquerda, sempre em frente.
Review today's words in the Words tab.

items: Recall the Portuguese words for train and bus.
Say 'the pharmacy is near' and 'the station is far'.
Say what you like and hate (yesterday's verbs).

activities.title: Lugares e direções
activities.intro: For location, Portuguese loves the verb 'ficar': 'Onde fica...?' = Where is...? Note the very European words: comboio (train) and autocarro (bus).

items.en: Where is the pharmacy? (ficar = to be located)
It's near. / It's far.
on/to the right / on/to the left
straight ahead
the train station — 'comboio' is the Portuguese word for train
the bus stop — 'autocarro' = bus in Portugal
the city centre

activities.title: Na cidade
activities.intro: Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: 'Comboio' means…
questions.explanation: In Portugal, train = comboio.

questions.prompt: Opposite of 'perto': 'A estação fica ____.' (far)
questions.explanation: perto = near; longe = far.

questions.prompt: Where do you wait for the autocarro?
questions.explanation: A paragem de autocarro = the bus stop.

questions.prompt: Put in order: 'Where is the train station?'
questions.explanation: Onde fica a estação de comboios?

questions.prompt: Give a direction: 'Vire à ____ no fim da rua.' (opposite of 'à direita')
questions.explanation: à esquerda = to the left; à direita = to the right.

questions.prompt: 'Sempre em frente' means…
questions.explanation: Siga sempre em frente = keep going straight ahead.

activities.title: Pedir direções
activities.intro: You ask a passer-by for directions. Read both parts aloud, then just the 'Me' lines.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Desculpe, onde fica a estação de comboios?
lines.en: Excuse me, where is the train station?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Fica perto. Siga sempre em frente.
lines.en: It's near. Go straight ahead.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sempre em frente. E depois?
lines.en: Straight ahead. And then?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Depois vire à esquerda, ao lado da farmácia.
lines.en: Then turn left, next to the pharmacy.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: É longe daqui?
lines.en: Is it far from here?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Não, são só cinco minutos a pé.
lines.en: No, it's only five minutes on foot.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Muito obrigado!
lines.en: Thank you very much!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: De nada. Bom dia!
lines.en: You're welcome. Have a good day!

days.title: Planos e ações
days.objective: Talk about future plans with 'ir + infinitivo' and about what is happening right now with the European 'estar a + infinitivo'.
days.paretoFocus: Two structures unlock time: 'vou fazer' = I'm going to do (future), 'estou a fazer' = I'm doing (now). The 'estar a + infinitive' form is the European Portuguese signature — Brazil says 'estou fazendo', Portugal says 'estou a fazer'.
drills: Say three plans for tomorrow with 'vou + infinitivo'.
Describe what you are doing right now with 'estou a + infinitivo'.
Review today's words in the Words tab.

items: Contrast aloud: 'Vou comer' vs 'Estou a comer'.
Say what you are going to do this weekend.
Ask where the bus stop is (yesterday's frame).

activities.title: Ir + infinitivo vs estar a + infinitivo
activities.intro: FUTURE PLAN: ir (conjugated) + infinitive — 'Vou estudar' = I'm going to study. HAPPENING NOW: estar (conjugated) + a + infinitive — 'Estou a estudar' = I am studying. This 'estar a' progressive is the European form; keep the two apart.

items.en: I'm going to study tomorrow. (ir + infinitive = future plan)
We're going to eat out on Saturday.
What are you going to do tomorrow? (informal)
I am studying now. (estar a + infinitive = happening right now)
She is working (at this moment).
Are you listening? (informal)
We are learning Portuguese.

activities.title: Planos e ações
activities.intro: Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: Which sentence means the action is happening RIGHT NOW?
questions.explanation: estar a + infinitive = in progress now; ir + infinitive = future plan.

questions.prompt: Future plan: 'Amanhã ____ visitar o museu.' (ir, 'eu' form)
questions.explanation: Eu form of ir: vou. Vou visitar = I'm going to visit.

questions.prompt: Complete the European progressive with the missing little word: 'Agora ela está ____ ler um livro.'
questions.explanation: European Portuguese: estar A + infinitive — está a ler = she is reading.

questions.prompt: The European Portuguese way to say 'I am working' is…
questions.explanation: Portugal uses estar a + infinitive; 'estou trabalhando' is the Brazilian gerund form.

questions.prompt: Put in order: 'What are you going to do at the weekend?'
questions.explanation: O que vais fazer no fim de semana?

questions.prompt: Happening now: 'Nós ____ a jantar.' (estar, 'nós' form)
questions.explanation: Nós form of estar: estamos. Estamos a jantar = we are having dinner.

questions.prompt: 'Vou comprar pão' refers to…
questions.explanation: ir + infinitive expresses what you are going to do.

activities.title: O que estás a fazer?
activities.intro: A phone call between friends. Read both parts aloud, then just the 'Me' lines.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Olá! O que estás a fazer?
lines.en: Hi! What are you doing?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Estou a estudar português.
lines.en: I'm studying Portuguese.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Que bom! E o que vais fazer amanhã?
lines.en: Great! And what are you going to do tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Amanhã vou visitar o centro da cidade.
lines.en: Tomorrow I'm going to visit the city centre.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Vais de comboio ou de autocarro?
lines.en: Are you going by train or by bus?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vou de autocarro. Queres vir?
lines.en: I'm going by bus. Do you want to come?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Quero! Vamos tomar um café juntos.
lines.en: I do! Let's have a coffee together.

days.title: A rotina diária
days.objective: Describe your daily routine with reflexive verbs, placing the pronoun after the verb (levanto-me) as European Portuguese does.
days.paretoFocus: In Portugal the reflexive pronoun normally comes AFTER the verb with a hyphen: levanto-me, deito-me, chamo-me. After 'não' and question words it jumps in front: 'Não me levanto', 'Como te chamas?'. Master this once and your routine talk sounds native.
drills: Narrate your morning aloud: levanto-me, lavo os dentes, tomo o pequeno-almoço.
Say the time you get up and the time you go to bed.
Review today's words in the Words tab.

items: Recall: where does the pronoun go in 'levanto-me'? And after 'não'?
Say the Portuguese word for breakfast.
Say what you are doing right now (yesterday's 'estar a').

activities.title: Verbos reflexos e a rotina
activities.intro: Reflexive verbs describe things you do to yourself. In European Portuguese the pronoun attaches AFTER the verb with a hyphen (ênclise): levanto-me. But after 'não' or a question word, it moves before the verb: 'Não me levanto cedo', 'Como te chamas?'.

items.en: My name is Ana. (literally: I call myself — pronoun after the verb)
I get up at seven.
I go to bed at midnight.
I have breakfast at eight — 'pequeno-almoço' is the Portuguese word for breakfast

items.hr: Lavo os dentes de manhã e à noite.
items.en: I brush my teeth in the morning and at night.
He gets up early.

items.hr: Como te chamas? / Não me levanto cedo.
items.en: What's your name? / I don't get up early. (after question words and 'não', the pronoun goes BEFORE the verb)

activities.title: A rotina
activities.intro: Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: '____-me às sete da manhã.' (levantar-se, 'eu' form — just the verb)
questions.explanation: Levanto-me às sete = I get up at seven; pronoun attached after the verb.

questions.prompt: The European Portuguese way to say 'I get up' is…
questions.explanation: In Portugal the pronoun follows the verb with a hyphen (ênclise); 'me levanto' as a sentence opener is Brazilian.

questions.prompt: 'O pequeno-almoço' is…
questions.explanation: In Portugal breakfast = o pequeno-almoço.

questions.prompt: 'Ela deita-____ tarde.' (the reflexive pronoun for ela)
questions.explanation: Ele/ela take 'se': ela deita-se tarde = she goes to bed late.

questions.prompt: Put in order: 'I get up at seven and have breakfast.'
questions.explanation: Levanto-me às sete e tomo o pequeno-almoço.

questions.prompt: After 'não' the pronoun moves before the verb: 'Não ____ levanto cedo ao domingo.'
questions.explanation: Negation attracts the pronoun: não me levanto = I don't get up.

questions.prompt: 'Deitar-se' means…
questions.explanation: Deito-me às onze = I go to bed at eleven.

activities.title: A tua rotina
activities.intro: Two colleagues compare routines. Read both parts aloud, then just the 'Me' lines.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A que horas te levantas?
lines.en: What time do you get up?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Levanto-me às sete e tomo o pequeno-almoço às sete e meia.
lines.en: I get up at seven and have breakfast at half past seven.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tão cedo! Eu não me levanto antes das nove.
lines.en: So early! I don't get up before nine.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: E a que horas te deitas?
lines.en: And what time do you go to bed?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Deito-me à meia-noite. E tu?
lines.en: I go to bed at midnight. And you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Deito-me às onze. Antes, lavo os dentes e leio um pouco.
lines.en: I go to bed at eleven. Before that, I brush my teeth and read a little.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Que rotina saudável!
lines.en: What a healthy routine!

days.title: A casa
days.objective: Name the rooms and main furniture of a home and describe it with 'há' and 'tem'.
days.paretoFocus: Six room words plus 'há' (there is) and 'tem' (it has) let you describe any home. Watch the European words: casa de banho (bathroom) and frigorífico (fridge).
drills: Name every room of your home aloud in Portuguese.
Describe your living room with two 'há' sentences.
Review today's words in the Words tab.

items: Recall the Portuguese for bathroom and fridge.
Say how many rooms your home has with 'tem'.
Narrate your morning routine (yesterday's verbs).

activities.title: As divisões da casa
activities.intro: The rooms of a house are 'as divisões'. To describe a home: 'há' = there is/are, 'tem' = (it) has. Note the very Portuguese words: casa de banho and frigorífico.

items.en: the bathroom — the standard word in Portugal
the bedroom
the kitchen / the fridge — 'frigorífico' in Portugal
the living room
the balcony
There is a sofa in the living room. (há = there is/are)
The house has three bedrooms. (ter for what something has)

activities.title: A casa
activities.intro: Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: 'A casa de banho' is…
questions.explanation: In Portugal the bathroom is a casa de banho.

questions.prompt: 'Na cozinha ____ um frigorífico.' (there is)
questions.explanation: Há = there is/are: na cozinha há um frigorífico.

questions.prompt: In Portugal, the fridge is…
questions.explanation: Portugal says frigorífico; 'a geleira' is a glacier, not a fridge.

questions.prompt: 'A casa ____ dois quartos e uma varanda.' (ter, 'ela' form)
questions.explanation: A casa tem = the house has.

questions.prompt: Put in order: 'There is a table and four chairs in the kitchen.'
questions.explanation: Há uma mesa e quatro cadeiras na cozinha.

questions.prompt: Where do you sleep?
questions.explanation: Durmo no quarto = I sleep in the bedroom.

activities.title: A casa nova
activities.intro: A friend asks about your new flat. Read both parts aloud, then just the 'Me' lines.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Então, como é a casa nova?
lines.en: So, what's the new place like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: É pequena mas bonita. Tem dois quartos e uma sala grande.
lines.en: It's small but pretty. It has two bedrooms and a big living room.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E a cozinha?
lines.en: And the kitchen?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A cozinha é nova. Há um frigorífico grande e uma mesa.
lines.en: The kitchen is new. There's a big fridge and a table.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tem varanda?
lines.en: Does it have a balcony?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tem, sim. E a casa de banho tem uma janela!
lines.en: It does. And the bathroom has a window!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Que sorte! Quero ver a casa em breve.
lines.en: Lucky you! I want to see the place soon.

days.title: Ter e haver
days.objective: Use 'ter' for possession and age, 'há' for existence, and 'ter de + infinitivo' for obligation.
days.paretoFocus: Three jobs, two verbs: ter = have (and AGE — 'tenho trinta anos'), há = there is/are, ter de = must. These cover possession, existence and obligation at A1.
drills: Say your age aloud with 'tenho ... anos'.
Say two things you have to do this week with 'tenho de'.
Review today's words in the Words tab.

items: Recall: which verb gives your age in Portuguese?
Say 'there is a café on the square'.
Describe your home with 'há' and 'tem' (yesterday).

activities.title: Ter, há, ter de
activities.intro: 'Ter' = to have: possessions AND age (never 'ser' for age). 'Há' (from haver) = there is / there are. 'Ter de + infinitivo' = to have to (obligation) — the preferred European form.

items.en: I have a car. (possession)
I am thirty years old. (age uses TER, literally 'I have thirty years')
How old are you? (informal)
There is a café on the square. (há = existence)
There is no bread at home.
I have to work tomorrow. (ter de + infinitive = obligation)
You have to try this cake!

activities.title: Ter e haver
activities.intro: Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: Say your age: 'Eu ____ vinte anos.' (ter, 'eu' form)
questions.explanation: Age uses ter: tenho vinte anos = I am twenty.

questions.prompt: To say your age in Portuguese you use the verb…
questions.explanation: Portuguese 'has' years: tenho trinta anos, never 'sou trinta anos'.

questions.prompt: '____ muitos restaurantes nesta rua.' (there are)
questions.explanation: Há is invariable: há um restaurante, há muitos restaurantes.

questions.prompt: 'Tenho de estudar' means…
questions.explanation: ter de + infinitive = obligation: I must/have to.

questions.prompt: Complete the obligation structure: 'Tens ____ falar com ela.' (the little word between the verb and the infinitive)
questions.explanation: Ter DE + infinitive: tens de falar = you have to talk.

questions.prompt: Put in order: 'I have to buy bread today.'
questions.explanation: Tenho de comprar pão hoje.

questions.prompt: Which sentence expresses EXISTENCE (there is)?
questions.explanation: Há = there is; ter = to possess; ter de = obligation.

activities.title: Um dia ocupado
activities.intro: Two friends compare busy days. Read both parts aloud, then just the 'Me' lines.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tens tempo para um café hoje?
lines.en: Do you have time for a coffee today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hoje não posso. Tenho de trabalhar até às seis.
lines.en: I can't today. I have to work until six.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E depois do trabalho?
lines.en: And after work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Depois tenho de ir ao supermercado. Não há pão em casa.
lines.en: Then I have to go to the supermarket. There's no bread at home.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Há um supermercado novo na praça. É muito bom.
lines.en: There's a new supermarket on the square. It's very good.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A sério? Então vou lá. E amanhã tomamos café?
lines.en: Really? Then I'll go there. And tomorrow we have coffee?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Combinado! Até amanhã.
lines.en: Deal! See you tomorrow.

days.title: Às compras
days.objective: Shop for food: ask prices with 'Quanto custa?', understand amounts in euros, and pay by card or cash.
days.paretoFocus: 'Quanto custa?' plus numbers and the payment words (multibanco, dinheiro, troco) get you through any shop in Portugal. 'Multibanco' is the Portuguese card/ATM network everyone mentions.
drills: Ask the price of three objects around you aloud.
Say three prices in euros aloud (e.g. 2,50 €).
Review today's words in the Words tab.

items: Recall how to ask 'how much does it cost?'.
Say 'Can I pay by card?' aloud.
Say two obligations with 'tenho de' (yesterday).

activities.title: Às compras
activities.intro: Shopping runs on a few fixed phrases. In Portugal you pay 'com multibanco' (the national debit-card network) or 'em dinheiro' (cash), and the change is 'o troco'.

items.en: How much does it cost?
It costs two euros fifty.
Can I pay by card? (multibanco = Portugal's debit-card/ATM network)
in cash
the change (money you get back)

items.hr: Queria meio quilo de queijo, se faz favor.
items.en: I'd like half a kilo of cheese, please.

items.hr: Mais alguma coisa? — É tudo, obrigado.
items.en: Anything else? — That's all, thank you.

activities.title: Às compras
activities.intro: Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: Ask the price: 'Quanto ____ este livro?' (custar, singular)
questions.explanation: Quanto custa? = how much does it cost?

questions.prompt: 'Posso pagar com multibanco?' means…
questions.explanation: Multibanco is Portugal's debit-card and ATM network; paying 'com multibanco' = by card.

questions.prompt: 'O troco' is…
questions.explanation: Aqui está o troco = here's your change.

questions.prompt: Pay in cash: 'Posso pagar ____ dinheiro?' (the preposition)
questions.explanation: Em dinheiro = in cash; com multibanco = by card.

questions.prompt: Put in order: 'How much does a kilo of apples cost?'
questions.explanation: Quanto custa um quilo de maçãs?

questions.prompt: The shopkeeper asks 'Mais alguma coisa?'. You have everything. You say…
questions.explanation: É tudo = that's all; the natural answer to 'anything else?'.

questions.prompt: 'Queria um ____ de tomates, se faz favor.' (the unit: 1000 grams)
questions.explanation: Um quilo = a kilo; meio quilo = half a kilo.

activities.title: No mercado
activities.intro: You buy fruit and cheese at the market. Read both parts aloud, then just the 'Me' lines.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bom dia! O que deseja?
lines.en: Good morning! What would you like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bom dia. Queria um quilo de maçãs, se faz favor.
lines.en: Good morning. I'd like a kilo of apples, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Com certeza. Mais alguma coisa?
lines.en: Of course. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sim, meio quilo de queijo. Quanto custa tudo?
lines.en: Yes, half a kilo of cheese. How much is everything?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: São sete euros e vinte.
lines.en: That's seven euros twenty.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Posso pagar com multibanco?
lines.en: Can I pay by card?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hoje não, desculpe. Só em dinheiro.
lines.en: Not today, sorry. Cash only.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Não faz mal. Aqui tem dez euros.
lines.en: No problem. Here's ten euros.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Obrigado. Aqui está o troco: dois euros e oitenta.
lines.en: Thank you. Here's your change: two euros eighty.

days.title: O tempo e a roupa
days.objective: Describe the weather (está sol, está a chover) and say what you wear, using 'vestir' for clothes and 'calçar' for shoes.
days.paretoFocus: Weather is 'está + ...' (está sol, está frio, está a chover — the European progressive again). Clothes split into two verbs: vestir for garments, calçar for footwear. 'Camisola' = sweater in Portugal.
drills: Describe today's weather aloud with 'está...'.
Say what you are wearing, using vestir and calçar.
Review today's words in the Words tab.

items: Say 'it's raining' the European way.
Recall: which verb goes with shoes?
Ask a price and pay by card (yesterday's phrases).

activities.title: O tempo e a roupa
activities.intro: Weather uses 'estar': está sol, está frio, está calor — and for rain the European progressive: está a chover. For clothes, Portuguese uses vestir; for shoes and anything on the feet, calçar.

items.en: It's sunny. / It's hot.
It's raining. (estar a + infinitive — the European progressive)
It's cold; I put on a coat. (vestir for clothes)
the sweater/jumper — the Portuguese word

items.hr: as calças / a saia / o vestido
items.en: the trousers / the skirt / the dress
I put on my shoes. (calçar for footwear)
the umbrella

activities.title: O tempo e a roupa
activities.intro: Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: The European Portuguese way to say 'it's raining' is…
questions.explanation: Portugal uses estar a + infinitive: está a chover; 'está chovendo' is Brazilian.

questions.prompt: 'Está frio: vou vestir um ____.' (the warm outer garment — a coat)
questions.explanation: Um casaco = a coat/jacket; visto um casaco quando está frio.

questions.prompt: For shoes, Portuguese uses the verb…
questions.explanation: Vestir for clothes, calçar for footwear: calço os sapatos.

questions.prompt: 'Eu ____ os sapatos antes de sair.' (calçar, 'eu' form)
questions.explanation: Eu calço os sapatos = I put on my shoes.

questions.prompt: In Portugal, 'a camisola' is…
questions.explanation: In Portugal camisola = sweater/jumper (in Brazil it means nightgown).

questions.prompt: Put in order: 'Today it's cold and it's raining.'
questions.explanation: Hoje está frio e está a chover.

questions.prompt: 'No verão ____ muito calor em Lisboa.' (estar, 3rd person)
questions.explanation: Weather with estar: está calor = it's hot.

activities.title: Que tempo está?
activities.intro: Two friends plan to go out despite the weather. Read both parts aloud, then just the 'Me' lines.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Vais sair? Está a chover!
lines.en: Are you going out? It's raining!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Não faz mal, levo o guarda-chuva.
lines.en: It's okay, I'll take the umbrella.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E está frio. O que vais vestir?
lines.en: And it's cold. What are you going to wear?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Visto uma camisola quente e um casaco.
lines.en: I'll put on a warm sweater and a coat.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E que sapatos vais calçar?
lines.en: And which shoes are you going to put on?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Calço as botas, claro.
lines.en: I'll put on my boots, of course.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Boa ideia. Amanhã vai estar sol!
lines.en: Good idea. Tomorrow it's going to be sunny!

days.title: Revisão A1 I
days.objective: Consolidate everything so far: ordering, likes, directions, plans, routine, home, ter/há, shopping and weather — and handle a full restaurant conversation.
days.paretoFocus: Review beats new material: the eight phrases below carry most daily interactions. If you can order a meal, ask a price, give your routine and say a plan, you are operational at A1.
drills: Say all eight recap phrases aloud from memory.
Role-play the restaurant dialogue alone, both parts.
Review this week's words in the Words tab.

items: Order a meal and ask for the bill aloud.
Contrast 'vou fazer' vs 'estou a fazer' with your own examples.
Say your morning routine in three sentences.

activities.title: As 8 frases de ouro
activities.intro: The eight highest-value phrases from the last ten days. If you own these, you can survive a day in Portugal. Say each aloud until it comes without thinking.

items.en: I'd like..., please. (orders anything, anywhere)
The bill, please.
How much does it cost?
Where is...? (any place)
I really like... (never forget the 'de')
I'm learning Portuguese. (the European progressive)
I have to go. (obligation with ter de)

items.hr: Levanto-me às sete e tomo o pequeno-almoço.
items.en: I get up at seven and have breakfast. (routine + ênclise)

activities.title: Revisão
activities.intro: Mixed questions over everything from lessons 1-9. Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: The polite way to order a coffee in Portugal:
options: Queria uma bica, se faz favor.
questions.answer: Queria uma bica, se faz favor.
questions.explanation: 'Queria..., se faz favor' is the universal polite ordering frame.

questions.prompt: Future plan: 'Amanhã ____ jantar fora.' (ir, 'eu' form)
questions.explanation: ir + infinitive for plans: vou jantar fora = I'm going to eat out.

questions.prompt: 'Ao fim de semana, deito-____ tarde.' (the reflexive pronoun for eu)
questions.explanation: Eu takes 'me', attached after the verb: deito-me tarde.

questions.prompt: A child says her age. Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Age uses ter: tenho oito anos = I am eight.

questions.prompt: Put in order: 'It's raining and I don't have an umbrella.'
questions.explanation: Está a chover e não tenho guarda-chuva.

questions.prompt: 'No meu quarto ____ uma cama grande.' (there is)
questions.explanation: Há = there is: no meu quarto há uma cama grande.

questions.prompt: You pay 10 € for something that costs 7,20 €. The 2,80 € you get back is…
questions.explanation: O troco = the change you get back.

questions.prompt: 'Nós ____ muito de bacalhau.' (gostar, 'nós' form)
questions.explanation: Nós form: gostamos — and gostar always with 'de'.

questions.prompt: The Portuguese (Portugal) word for breakfast is…
questions.explanation: In Portugal breakfast is o pequeno-almoço — a fixed compound, never reversed.

activities.title: No restaurante
activities.intro: A full restaurant visit — greeting, ordering, small talk and the bill. Read both parts aloud, then just the 'Me' lines.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Boa tarde! Uma mesa para quantas pessoas?
lines.en: Good afternoon! A table for how many people?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Boa tarde. Para uma pessoa, se faz favor.
lines.en: Good afternoon. For one person, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Com certeza. Aqui está o menu. O que deseja beber?
lines.en: Of course. Here's the menu. What would you like to drink?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Queria um sumo de laranja, se faz favor.
lines.en: I'd like an orange juice, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E para comer? O peixe hoje está muito bom.
lines.en: And to eat? The fish is very good today.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gosto muito de peixe. Quanto custa o bacalhau?
lines.en: I really like fish. How much is the cod?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Custa doze euros e vem com batatas e legumes.
lines.en: It costs twelve euros and comes with potatoes and vegetables.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perfeito, então queria o bacalhau.
lines.en: Perfect, then I'd like the cod.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Muito bem. Está a gostar de Portugal?
lines.en: Very well. Are you enjoying Portugal?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Estou a adorar. Estou a aprender português!
lines.en: I'm loving it. I'm learning Portuguese!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E fala muito bem! Mais alguma coisa?
lines.en: And you speak very well! Anything else?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Não, é tudo. A conta, se faz favor.
lines.en: No, that's all. The bill, please.

days.title: Transportes
days.objective: Get around Lisbon or Porto by bus, metro, train and tram, and buy the right ticket.
days.paretoFocus: Four vehicle words (autocarro, metro, comboio, elétrico) plus 'apanhar/perder' and 'um bilhete para…' cover almost every daily transport situation in Portugal.
drills: Say aloud how you get to work: 'Apanho o autocarro / o metro…'.
Role-play buying a ticket: 'Queria um bilhete para…, por favor.'
Review today's transport words in the Words tab.

items: Recall the pt-PT words for bus, train and tram.
Say 'I missed the bus!' in Portuguese.
Ask what time the next train leaves.

activities.title: Na paragem e na estação
activities.intro: European Portuguese has its own transport words — learn these and you will sound local from day one. Say each aloud.

items.en: the bus — 'Apanho o autocarro 728.' (I catch the 728 bus.)
the train — 'O comboio para o Porto parte da estação de Santa Apolónia.'
the underground — Lisbon and Porto both have one; 'a linha azul' (the blue line)
the tram — the famous yellow elétrico 28 climbs the hills of Lisbon
the ticket / the travel pass — you must validate (validar) it when boarding
to catch / to miss the bus — 'Perdi o autocarro!' (I missed the bus!)
the (bus) stop / the station — 'Espero na paragem.' (I wait at the stop.)

activities.title: Apanhar o autocarro
activities.intro: Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: Which is the European Portuguese word for 'train'?
questions.explanation: O comboio = the train. Um camião is a lorry, uma carrinha is a van.

questions.prompt: The famous yellow tram that climbs the hills of Lisbon is…
questions.explanation: O elétrico is the tram — line 28 is the classic Lisbon ride.

questions.prompt: 'To catch the bus' in European Portuguese is…
questions.explanation: Apanhar = to catch; perder = to miss.

questions.prompt: Where do you wait for the bus?
questions.explanation: A paragem (de autocarro) = the bus stop.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Cheguei tarde e perdi o ___!' (the bus — one word)
questions.explanation: Perder o autocarro = to miss the bus. Never the Brazilian word — in Portugal it is always 'autocarro'.

questions.prompt: At the ticket office: 'Queria um ___ para o Porto, por favor.' (a ticket)
questions.explanation: Um bilhete = a ticket. 'Queria…' is the polite way to ask.

questions.prompt: Put in order: ask what time the train leaves.
questions.explanation: A que horas parte o comboio? = What time does the train leave?

activities.title: Na bilheteira
activities.intro: Buying a train ticket at Santa Apolónia station in Lisbon. Read both parts aloud, then just the 'Me' lines.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bom dia. Faz favor?
lines.en: Good morning. How can I help you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bom dia. Queria um bilhete para o Porto, por favor.
lines.en: Good morning. I'd like a ticket to Porto, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ida e volta?
lines.en: Return (there and back)?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Só ida. A que horas parte o próximo comboio?
lines.en: One way only. What time does the next train leave?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Parte às dez e meia, da linha três.
lines.en: It leaves at half past ten, from platform three.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Quanto custa?
lines.en: How much does it cost?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: São vinte e cinco euros.
lines.en: That's twenty-five euros.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Aqui está. Obrigado, bom dia!
lines.en: Here you are. Thank you, good day!

days.title: Tu, você ou o senhor?
days.objective: Choose the right level of formality with friends, at the bakery counter and in a formal office — the European address system in practice.
days.paretoFocus: In Portugal the verb ending does the work: 'Queres…?' to a friend, 'Quer…?' or 'O senhor quer…?' to a stranger. Avoid saying 'você' out loud — it can sound blunt.
drills: Address 5 imaginary people (friend, baker, boss, child, elderly neighbour) with the right form.
Say 'Desculpa' vs 'Desculpe' aloud and picture who each is for.
Review today's words in the Words tab.

items: Recall: how do you ask a close friend if they want a coffee?
Recall: how do you ask an elderly stranger the same thing?
Why is the bare word 'você' often avoided in Portugal?

activities.title: Os três registos
activities.intro: European Portuguese has a finely tuned system of address. You rarely need the pronoun itself — the verb form and 'o senhor / a senhora' carry the register.

items.en: informal 'you': friends, family, young people, colleagues your age — verb in 2nd person

items.hr: o senhor / a senhora — O senhor quer um café?
items.en: formal and polite: strangers, older people, customers — verb in 3rd person
exists but is often avoided in Portugal — said directly it can sound blunt; drop the pronoun instead
the safest neutral-formal form: just the 3rd-person verb, no pronoun at all
sorry / excuse me — Desculpa to a friend (tu), Desculpe to anyone formal

items.hr: o Miguel, a Ana — A Ana quer vir?
items.en: semi-formal: addressing someone by article + first name, verb in 3rd person — very Portuguese

activities.title: Que registo?
activities.intro: Pick the right level of formality.

questions.prompt: You ask an elderly stranger for help. Which is correct?
questions.explanation: With an elderly stranger, use 'o senhor / a senhora' + 3rd person.

questions.prompt: You offer a coffee to a close friend. Which is correct?
questions.explanation: With friends use tu: queres (2nd person singular).

questions.prompt: In Portugal, saying 'você' directly to someone's face often sounds…
questions.explanation: The Portuguese prefer the bare 3rd-person verb or 'o senhor / a senhora' instead of saying 'você'.

questions.prompt: 'A senhora deseja mais alguma coisa?' — who is being addressed?
questions.explanation: A senhora = formal address for a woman; deseja = 3rd person.

questions.prompt: You bump into a stranger. Apologise formally: '___!' (one word)
questions.explanation: Desculpe = formal apology; to a friend you would use the tu form instead.

questions.prompt: To a friend: '(Tu) ___ ir ao cinema?' (do you want)
questions.explanation: Tu queres — 2nd person singular of querer.

questions.prompt: Put in order: a formal question to a man.
questions.explanation: O senhor é daqui? = Are you from here? (formal)

activities.title: Na padaria e na rua
activities.intro: First a formal exchange at the bakery counter, then an informal one with a friend outside. Notice how the verb forms switch.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bom dia! O que deseja?
lines.en: Good morning! What would you like? (formal)

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bom dia. Queria dois pães e um pastel de nata, faz favor.
lines.en: Good morning. I'd like two bread rolls and a custard tart, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Com certeza. Deseja mais alguma coisa?
lines.en: Certainly. Would you like anything else?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Não, obrigado. Quanto é?
lines.en: No, thank you. How much is it?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: São dois euros e dez. Aqui tem, bom dia!
lines.en: That's two euros ten. Here you are, good day!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Olá, Rita! Tu por aqui?
lines.en: Hi, Rita! You, around here? (now informal — a friend outside)

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Olá! Queres tomar um café comigo?
lines.en: Hi! Do you want to have a coffee with me?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Pode ser! Vamos.
lines.en: Sounds good! Let's go.

days.title: Isto, isso, aquilo
days.objective: Point at the world with the three-way demonstrative system (isto/isso/aquilo, este/esse/aquele) and round up the possessives.
days.paretoFocus: Portuguese splits space in three: near me (isto/cá), near you (isso/aí), far from both (aquilo/lá). Master the triangle once and every shop conversation gets easier.
drills: Point at 3 objects: one near you (isto), one near someone else (isso), one far away (aquilo).
Say 5 possessions aloud: 'o meu livro, a minha casa…'.
Review today's words in the Words tab.

items: Recall the triangle: isto / isso / aquilo — whose space is each?
Match cá, aí, lá to the three demonstratives.
Say 'my mother' and 'our house' with the article.

activities.title: O triângulo do espaço
activities.intro: Three distances, three sets of words. The neutral forms (isto, isso, aquilo) point at things you can't name yet; este/esse/aquele agree with a noun.

items.en: this (near me) — 'O que é isto?' (What is this?)
that (near YOU) — 'Isso que tens na mão é um azulejo.'
that over there (far from both) — 'Aquilo ali é o castelo.'

items.hr: este / esta, esse / essa, aquele / aquela
items.en: with a noun they agree in gender: este livro, essa mesa, aquela casa
the matching place words: cá (here, by me), aí (there, by you), lá (over there)

items.hr: o meu / a minha, o teu / a tua
items.en: my, your (informal) — with the article in pt-PT: 'o meu pai, a tua mãe'

items.hr: o nosso / a nossa, o seu / a sua
items.en: our; his/her/your(formal) — 'a nossa casa' (our house)

activities.title: Perto ou longe?
activities.intro: Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: The other person is holding something. You ask: 'O que é ___?'
questions.explanation: Isso = near the person you are talking to (aí).

questions.prompt: Something far from both of you is…
questions.explanation: Aquilo = far from speaker and listener (lá/ali).

questions.prompt: '___ livro aqui é muito interessante.'
questions.explanation: 'Aqui' signals the speaker's space, so este.

questions.prompt: The place word 'aí' means…
questions.explanation: cá = my space, aí = your space, lá = far from both.

questions.prompt: You hold something up and ask: 'O que é ___?' (this thing, near me)
questions.explanation: Isto = neutral demonstrative for the speaker's space.

questions.prompt: Complete with the possessive: 'É a ___ mãe.' (my)
questions.explanation: Feminine noun (mãe) → a minha. pt-PT keeps the article: 'a minha mãe'.

questions.prompt: Put in order: say whose car that is.
questions.explanation: Aquele carro é do meu pai. = That car (over there) is my father's.

activities.title: Na loja de artesanato
activities.intro: Pointing at things in a craft shop. Watch isto/isso/aquilo move around the room.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Boa tarde. Posso ajudar?
lines.en: Good afternoon. Can I help?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sim, faz favor. O que é aquilo ali na montra?
lines.en: Yes, please. What is that over there in the shop window?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Aquilo é um galo de Barcelos, uma peça tradicional.
lines.en: That is a Barcelos rooster, a traditional piece.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: E isso aí ao seu lado?
lines.en: And that thing there next to you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Isto? É um azulejo pintado à mão.
lines.en: This? It's a hand-painted tile.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Que bonito! E este azulejo aqui, quanto custa?
lines.en: How pretty! And this tile here, how much does it cost?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Esse custa dez euros.
lines.en: That one costs ten euros.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Então levo este. Obrigado!
lines.en: Then I'll take this one. Thank you!

days.title: Plural e concordância
days.objective: Form plurals — including the tricky -ão words (limões, pães, mãos) — and make adjectives agree in descriptions.
days.paretoFocus: Most plurals just add -s or -es. The -ão family splits three ways (-ões, -ães, -ãos); learn one example of each and the pattern sticks.
drills: Pluralise aloud: pão, limão, mão, animal, flor.
Describe 3 things around you with full agreement: 'as cadeiras pretas…'.
Review today's words in the Words tab.

items: Recall the three plurals of -ão words with one example each.
Pluralise: animal, papel.
Say 'the white houses' with full agreement.

activities.title: Um pão, dois pães
activities.intro: Plural rules from the regular to the famous exceptions — plus the golden rule: article, noun and adjective all agree.

items.en: words ending in a vowel: just add -s
words ending in -r or -s: add -es
MOST -ão words go to -ões — the biggest group

items.hr: pão → pães, cão → cães, alemão → alemães
items.en: a small famous group goes to -ães
and a few just add -s: -ãos
words in -l: drop the l, add -is

items.hr: as casas brancas, os olhos azuis
items.en: agreement: article + noun + adjective all match in gender and number

activities.title: Plurais
activities.intro: Choose or complete.

questions.prompt: The plural of 'pão' (bread) is…
questions.explanation: pão belongs to the small -ães group: pão → pães, cão → cães.

questions.prompt: The plural of 'limão' (lemon) is…
questions.explanation: Most -ão words go to -ões: limão → limões.

questions.prompt: The plural of 'animal' is…
questions.explanation: Words in -l drop the l and add -is: animal → animais.
